title: Lifecycle commands can launch shadow config trees while canonical config digests still certify the phase packet
protected_asset: canonical HJMT runtime topology integrity and release-evidence authenticity
trust_boundary: operator-authored lifecycle YAML and manifest fields -> aggregator OS process launch and public release packet
attacker_capability_model: a malicious operator or compromised deployment artifact can rewrite start_cmd and restart_cmd while keeping the canonical config tree present on disk
main_vulnerability: Lifecycle command validation is marker-based rather than path-bound, so start_cmd and restart_cmd can point to shadow aggregator-config.yaml, planner-config.yaml, and storage-config.yaml files while preflight and release evidence continue hashing and certifying the canonical config files under hjmt.home.
implementation_nuance: NodeConfig hashes the canonical config home, while lifecycle validation and runtime observability only check for marker substrings in the launch commands. That leaves a shadow-config execution lane that can diverge from the recorded digest set.

evidence.summary: Phase 058 makes five OS-process launches plus explicit config-digest evidence a release gate.
config_digests() hashes only the canonical files resolved from hjmt.home.
Lifecycle validation accepts commands based on has_cfg_refs() instead of exact path binding.
has_cfg_refs() only checks filename markers and agg-N substrings.
The release packet reuses canonical config digests from node_cfg.config_digests().
Process-topology validation only checks config-marker presence, not exact launch targets.

pros: exact string-only validation is visible in code
Phase 058 scope explicitly forbids parallel truth lanes
cons: impact is integrity and evidence-authenticity drift rather than a direct proof bypass

verification.reason: The candidate exceeded the admission threshold with concrete production-code evidence across the config/evidence trust boundary.

defenses: parse lifecycle commands and bind config flags to the exact canonical paths
reject runtime packet process rows whose launch commands do not match the hashed config trio
add regression coverage for shadow-path lifecycle commands that preserve filename markers
residual_risk: Authorized operators can still change the canonical configs themselves; the hardening removes the hidden shadow-config lane rather than all insider risk.
